bytes.topic: Why Queues — decoupling, async processing, load leveling
bytes.title: BullMQ — Why Queues Beat Direct Calls for Async Workloads
bytes.body:
When your Express route directly calls sendEmail() or generatePDF(), you've coupled request latency to processing time and tied your API's availability to every downstream dependency. BullMQ breaks that coupling by turning side effects into durable jobs.

With BullMQ, the route adds a job to a Queue and returns immediately — the Worker picks it up independently, in a separate process or even a separate machine. If the email provider is down, the job sits in Redis and retries with exponential backoff via the `attempts` and `backoff` options. Your API stays responsive.

Load leveling is the other win. A viral post might trigger 10,000 notification jobs in seconds. Without a queue, your worker pool drowns. With BullMQ, you set `concurrency` on the Worker and the queue absorbs the spike — jobs are processed at the rate your infrastructure can handle, not the rate users generate them.

BullMQ also gives you `priority`, rate limiting via `limiter`, and job deduplication with custom `jobId` — primitives that turn a naive fire-and-forget pattern into a production-grade async pipeline.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — designing consumers to safely handle duplicate messages
bytes.title: BullMQ Idempotency — Designing Consumers That Survive Duplicate Jobs
bytes.body:
BullMQ's first deduplication layer is the `jobId` option on `queue.add()`. If a job with that ID already exists in `waiting`, `active`, or `delayed` state, the new add is silently ignored — no duplicate processing. But once a job completes and is removed (default behavior), that ID is free again. Set `removeOnComplete: false` to keep completed job records and block re-adds at the queue level.

For consumer-side safety, your processor must also be idempotent. Use `job.id` as an idempotency key against a Redis SET or a DB unique constraint. Check before doing work, record after. If your job triggers an external side effect (payment, email), guard it with a distributed lock using `ioredis` or an idempotency record in Postgres before calling out.

Avoid relying on `job.attemptsMade === 0` as a dedup signal — retries increment that counter but the original side effect may have partially succeeded. Track completion state externally, keyed by `job.id`, and return early if already processed. This pattern survives worker crashes, Redis failovers, and upstream replay storms.

bytes.topic: Dead Letter Queues — poison message handling, retry strategies with backoff
bytes.title: BullMQ Dead Letter Queues — Poison Message Handling and Backoff Strategies
bytes.body:
BullMQ doesn't ship a native dead-letter queue — you build one. When a job exhausts all retry attempts it lands in the queue's failed set, inspectable via `queue.getFailed()`. The signal to route it elsewhere is the Worker's `failed` event, checked against `job.attemptsMade >= (job.opts.attempts ?? 1)`.

Before a job gets there, configure retry behaviour per-job via `attempts` and `backoff`. BullMQ supports two built-in backoff strategies: `fixed` (constant delay in ms) and `exponential` (delay doubles each attempt, seeded by `delay` ms). Exponential is the right default for transient failures — downstream unavailability, rate limits, lock contention.

Poison messages are a different problem: jobs that fail regardless of retries due to a bad payload or schema mismatch. Re-adding them to the same queue just burns workers. Route them to a dedicated `orders:dlq` queue on final failure, preserving `job.data` and appending the error string. From there you can inspect, replay selectively, or trigger an alert.

Set `removeOnFail: false` to keep failed jobs in Redis for auditing, or `removeOnFail: { count: 500 }` in production to cap memory growth.

bytes.topic: Why Caching — reducing DB load, latency, cache-aside pattern
bytes.title: BullMQ + Cache-Aside — Reuse Your Redis Connection to Crush DB Load
bytes.body:
BullMQ workers already depend on Redis — every Queue and Worker you instantiate is backed by an ioredis connection. That means caching costs you nothing extra in infrastructure.

The problem: high-throughput workers often fetch the same DB rows repeatedly. A send-notification worker processing 50k jobs/hour that queries user preferences on every job is hammering Postgres with identical reads. The fix is cache-aside using the same ioredis instance BullMQ already holds.

Pattern: on each job, check Redis for the cache key. On a hit, deserialize and proceed. On a miss, query the DB, serialize, write to Redis with a TTL (`SET key value EX 60`), then proceed. The TTL prevents stale data from accumulating indefinitely.

Pass the same `connection` instance to both your `Worker` constructor and your cache reads/writes. BullMQ spawns separate internal clients per role (blocking client for `BRPOPLPUSH`, non-blocking for everything else), but your shared ioredis instance runs alongside them safely.

For lookup-heavy workers, a 30–60s TTL on stable data like user configs or feature flags can reduce DB load by 90%+ with zero added latency on cache hits.

bytes.topic: Message Delivery Guarantees — at-least-once vs at-most-once vs exactly-once
bytes.title: BullMQ Delivery Guarantees — at-least-once by Default, How to Go Further
bytes.body:
BullMQ's default model is at-least-once. When a Worker dequeues a job, it acquires a Redis lock (lockDuration, default 30 s). If the process dies before completion, the lock expires and the built-in StalledJobsChecker (v2+, previously QueueScheduler) re-queues the job — tunable via stalledInterval and maxStalledCount. With attempts > 1, the job retries automatically on failure.

at-most-once has no native mode. Approximate it by calling await job.remove() before your processing logic. You forfeit all retry guarantees — use only when duplicate side-effects are more damaging than data loss.

exactly-once is impossible to guarantee end-to-end, but BullMQ provides two complementary tools:

1. Enqueue deduplication — pass a fixed jobId. If a job with that ID is already waiting or active, queue.add() returns the existing job silently instead of creating a duplicate.
2. Processor idempotency — inside the worker, use a Redis SET NX keyed on job.id to guard against double-processing when a stalled job is re-delivered.

The separation matters: BullMQ owns delivery semantics; your processor owns idempotency.

bytes.topic: BullMQ — job priorities, rate limiting, repeatable jobs, job dependencies
bytes.title: BullMQ — Priorities, Rate Limiting, Repeatables, and Job Dependencies
bytes.body: BullMQ gives you fine-grained control over job execution through four orthogonal mechanisms. Job priorities (1–2,097,152, lower = higher priority) let critical work jump the queue without a separate queue. Rate limiting via `limiter: { max, duration }` on the Worker caps throughput at the worker level — useful for third-party API quotas. Repeatable jobs use a cron expression or fixed interval stored in a sorted set; only one schedule entry exists regardless of how many times you call `add()` with the same `repeat` config and `jobId`. Job dependencies via `parent` and `children` create a DAG: a Flow produces parent-child chains where the parent job only moves to active once all children complete. Children can themselves have children. Use `FlowProducer.add()` to define the tree in one atomic call. One gotcha: repeatable jobs with `removeOnComplete` set to `true` still persist the repeat key — you must call `queue.removeRepeatable()` explicitly to stop recurrence. Mixing priorities with flows works, but priority is evaluated per-queue, so child jobs in different queues won't inherit parent priority.

bytes.topic: Kafka — partitions, consumer groups, offset management, log compaction
bytes.title: BullMQ vs Kafka — Partitions, Consumer Groups, and Log Compaction Mapped
bytes.body:
Kafka distributes load via partitions; BullMQ achieves similar isolation through named queues. Spin up a `notifications-high` and `notifications-low` queue instead of partition keys — each gets its own Redis key namespace and independent throughput. For consumer groups, BullMQ Workers compete for jobs on a single queue using Redis' atomic `BRPOPLPUSH` under the hood, so concurrency is configured per-worker instance via `{ concurrency: 5 }`. Run multiple worker processes pointing at the same queue name for horizontal scaling — BullMQ handles the fan-out safely without duplicate processing.

Offset management in Kafka is explicit; BullMQ tracks job state transitions implicitly in Redis sorted sets (`waiting`, `active`, `completed`, `failed`). Use `queue.getJobs(['completed'], 0, 100)` to inspect a window of completed jobs — the closest analog to rewinding a consumer offset.

For log compaction: BullMQ has no key-based deduplication like Kafka, but `removeOnComplete: { count: 1000 }` and `removeOnFail: { age: 86400 }` keep Redis memory bounded. Pair with `queue.obliterate()` for full resets during blue-green deploys.

bytes.topic: Redis as Cache — TTL, eviction policies, cache-aside vs write-through vs write-behind
bytes.title: BullMQ + Redis Cache — TTL, Eviction Policies, and the Patterns That Actually Hold Up
bytes.body:
BullMQ stores all job state in Redis — active sets, waiting lists, locks, rate-limiter keys. Mixing cache workloads into the same Redis instance creates a dangerous interaction: your eviction policy.

Set `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru` and Redis will evict BullMQ's internal `bull:*` keys under memory pressure, silently dropping jobs. Use `volatile-lru` instead and ensure only your cache keys carry a TTL — BullMQ's queue keys are TTL-free by default, making them safe from volatile eviction.

For job result caching, the cache-aside pattern fits best inside a Worker handler: check Redis before processing, populate on miss with `SET key value EX <ttl>`. Write-through works too — write to the cache synchronously before returning from the job handler. Write-behind is risky: BullMQ's `removeOnComplete: { age: 3600 }` controls job record lifetime only, not your cache keys. A crashed worker leaves stale cache entries indefinitely.

Never share BullMQ's internal IORedis connection for cache ops — BullMQ pipelines commands through it and subscribing or blocking on the same client causes dropped events. Instantiate a separate `ioredis` client. Audit key heat with `OBJECT IDLETIME` and `OBJECT FREQ` before tuning `maxmemory`.

bytes.topic: Redis as Queue — pub/sub vs streams vs lists, persistence trade-offs
bytes.title: BullMQ — Redis Lists, Sorted Sets, and Streams: What Actually Backs Your Queue
bytes.body:
BullMQ stores jobs in Redis Lists (`bull:{name}:wait`, `bull:{name}:active`) using LPUSH/BRPOP — not pub/sub. Delayed and prioritized jobs live in Sorted Sets, scored by execution timestamp or priority value. This is why `queue.getDelayed()` returns jobs in precise schedule order.

For events, BullMQ deliberately chose Redis Streams over pub/sub (the approach Bull v3 used). Streams persist messages in `bull:{name}:events`, so a `QueueEvents` subscriber that restarts can replay missed completions, failures, and stalls — pub/sub would have silently dropped them.

The persistence trade-off: Lists and Sorted Sets survive Redis restarts only if AOF (`appendonly yes`) or RDB snapshots are enabled. BullMQ adds no WAL of its own — it fully delegates durability to Redis. For critical workloads, `appendfsync everysec` is the minimum safe setting.

`removeOnComplete` and `removeOnFail` control Sorted Set bloat. Setting them to `{ count: 1000 }` caps memory without losing all observability. Leaving both false on high-throughput queues causes unbounded growth in `bull:{name}:completed` and `bull:{name}:failed`.

bytes.topic: RabbitMQ — exchanges, routing keys, acknowledgements, prefetch count
bytes.title: BullMQ — Routing Keys, Acks, and Prefetch Without RabbitMQ's Complexity
bytes.body:
BullMQ skips RabbitMQ's exchange/binding layer entirely — queues are addressed directly by name. Job names serve as routing keys: a single Worker on the `orders` queue can branch on `job.name` to replicate topic-exchange fan-out without extra infrastructure or bindings to maintain.

Acknowledgements are implicit and lock-guarded. When your Worker handler resolves, BullMQ internally calls `moveToCompleted`, holding a unique per-job token to prevent double-processing across distributed workers. Throwing rejects the job — it moves to `failed` or re-queues via `opts.attempts` + `backoff`. There is no manual `ack()`/`nack()` call; the resolved or rejected promise *is* the ack.

Prefetch maps directly to the `concurrency` option on `Worker`. Setting `concurrency: 5` means at most 5 jobs are active in-flight per worker process. Unlike RabbitMQ's channel-level prefetch, BullMQ's concurrency is scoped to the `Worker` instance — scale throughput by spawning additional processes, not by widening a single connection's window.

bytes.topic: Celery — task routing, beat scheduler, result backends, task chaining
bytes.title: BullMQ — Routing, Cron Scheduling, Result Access, and FlowProducer Chains
bytes.body:
BullMQ handles task routing through named queues — workers bind to a specific queue via `new Worker('email-queue', processor)`, so routing is implicit in which `Queue` instance you call `.add()` on. There's no global routing config like Celery's `task_routes`; queue name IS the route.

For scheduling (Celery beat equivalent), BullMQ uses repeatable jobs. Pass `{ repeat: { pattern: '0 9 * * *' } }` directly in `queue.add()`. In BullMQ v5+, the scheduler is built into `Queue` itself — no separate `QueueScheduler` process needed.

Result backends are Redis-native. When a worker's processor resolves a value, BullMQ persists it automatically. Retrieve it with `queue.getJob(jobId)` and read `.returnvalue` — no separate backend configuration required unlike Celery's `CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND`.

For chaining, `FlowProducer` is the answer — and it's more powerful than Celery's `chain()`. You define a job DAG where children must complete before the parent runs. Parent jobs receive children's results via `job.getChildrenValues()`, enabling true pipeline composition across multiple queues.

bytes.topic: Ordering — when order matters, partitioning strategy for ordered processing
bytes.title: BullMQ Ordering — Partitioning Strategy for Sequential Job Processing
bytes.body:
BullMQ doesn't guarantee ordering when `concurrency > 1` — multiple workers racing on the same queue will process jobs out of sequence. For strict ordered processing (per-user event streams, sequential file transforms, ledger operations), partition by a logical key into separate queues, each backed by a `Worker` with `concurrency: 1`.

At runtime, maintain a registry of queue/worker pairs keyed by your partition identifier (userId, tenantId, deviceId). When a job arrives, map it to a queue name like `stream:user-42`, add the job, and lazily spin up a `Worker` for that queue if one doesn't exist. Each worker sees only its own FIFO stream — no concurrency races, no reordering.

Watch out: this creates O(N) Redis list structures and O(N) in-process event loops. Cap your partition count or reuse workers across a bounded pool of queue names. For high-cardinality keys, use a consistent-hash ring to map many keys to a smaller set of ordered queues — but always validate those workers run at `concurrency: 1`. Never raise concurrency thinking rate limiting will preserve order; it won't.

bytes.topic: NATS — JetStream, subject-based routing, at-least-once delivery
bytes.title: BullMQ vs NATS JetStream — Subject Routing and At-Least-Once Delivery Mapped
bytes.body:
NATS JetStream offers subject-based routing (`orders.>`, `payments.*`) and durable at-least-once delivery. BullMQ provides equivalent guarantees through different primitives worth understanding side-by-side.

In BullMQ, queue names act as your top-level subject namespace, and job names provide the fine-grained routing JetStream subjects give you. A single Worker processes a queue but branches on `job.name` — no separate queue per event type required.

At-least-once delivery in JetStream hinges on explicit `ack()` calls within `ackWait`. BullMQ maps this to `lockDuration`: a Worker holds a Redis lock on the job for that window. If the worker crashes or stalls beyond `lockDuration`, the stalled-job checker (running every `stalledInterval` ms) re-queues the job automatically — up to `maxStalledCount` times before moving it to failed.

The critical difference: JetStream consumers can be push or pull; BullMQ is always pull — workers poll Redis. This means BullMQ won't saturate downstream services on burst traffic the way a push consumer can, but you lose JetStream's server-side fan-out to multiple consumer groups on a single subject stream.

bytes.topic: Scaling — horizontal consumer scaling, partition count, rebalancing
bytes.title: BullMQ — Horizontal Worker Scaling, Locking, and Stall Recovery
bytes.body:
Running multiple BullMQ `Worker` instances against the same queue is all you need for horizontal scaling — Redis handles coordination atomically. Each worker claims a job via a Lua script that moves it from the `wait` sorted set to `active` in one atomic operation, preventing double-processing without any external locking ceremony.

Throughput = worker instances × `concurrency`. The `concurrency` option (default: 1) sets parallel job slots per worker process. On I/O-heavy workloads, push it high — 20–50 is common. On CPU-bound work, keep it near core count.

BullMQ has no Kafka-style partitions to rebalance. All workers poll the same Redis key, so distribution is naturally load-balanced — an idle worker picks up the next available job immediately.

The critical tuning surface is `lockDuration` (default: 30 000 ms). A crashed worker releases its lock only after this window expires. Set `stalledInterval` to less than half of `lockDuration` so the stall checker catches orphaned jobs promptly. `maxStalledCount` (default: 1) controls how many stall cycles a job survives before being moved to `failed`. For long-running jobs, use `job.updateProgress()` — it also renews the lock, preventing false stalls.

bytes.topic: Backpressure — what happens when consumers lag, preventing memory blowout
bytes.title: BullMQ Backpressure — Stopping Memory Blowout When Consumers Lag
bytes.body:
BullMQ stores jobs in Redis sorted sets and lists. When producers outpace workers, the `waiting` list grows unchecked until Redis hits `maxmemory` and starts evicting keys — or crashes outright. BullMQ has no built-in flow control; you own the backpressure logic.

`queue.getJobCounts('waiting', 'active')` is your pressure gauge. Before each `queue.add()`, check whether the waiting depth exceeds a safe threshold. If it does, back off and retry. This is a simple, producer-side throttle that costs one Redis call per enqueue.

On the worker side, `concurrency` is your primary lever. For I/O-bound jobs, push it to 20–50; for CPU-bound work, cap it at `os.cpus().length`. Higher concurrency drains the waiting list faster but increases in-flight Redis reads.

The silent killer: completed and failed jobs. Without `removeOnComplete` and `removeOnFail` on your Worker, every finished job stays in Redis forever. A queue processing 10k jobs/day accumulates millions of stale entries within weeks. Set count-based retention on every worker, no exceptions.

For event-driven pipelines, listen to `QueueEvents` `'drained'` to resume a paused producer the moment the queue empties, avoiding busy-wait polling.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — queue depth alerts, consumer lag, dead letter queue alerts
bytes.title: BullMQ Monitoring — Queue Depth Alerts, Consumer Lag, and Dead Letter Queues
bytes.body: BullMQ exposes rich observability via `Queue.getJobCounts()`, which returns active, waiting, failed, delayed, and paused counts in a single Redis call. Poll this on an interval and alert when `waiting` exceeds your SLA threshold or when `failed` crosses a noise floor. Consumer lag — the gap between job `timestamp` and `processedOn` — is available per-job via `Job.asJSON()` or in aggregate by comparing `Queue.getWaitingCount()` against worker throughput sampled over time. For dead-letter semantics, BullMQ has no built-in DLQ, but you can simulate one with `Worker`'s `failed` event: move exhausted jobs (where `job.attemptsMade >= job.opts.attempts`) to a dedicated `failed-jobs` queue using `failedQueue.add(job.name, job.data)`. Pair this with `QueueEvents` to receive `failed` and `stalled` events without polling. Set `stalledInterval` and `maxStalledCount` on the `Worker` to auto-recover or promote stalled jobs. Use `FlowProducer` job trees? Check parent job state via `Job.getDependenciesCount()` to detect dependency bottlenecks before the whole flow stalls.

bytes.topic: Security — broker authentication, TLS, network isolation
bytes.title: BullMQ Security — Locking Down Redis Auth, TLS, and Network Isolation
bytes.body: BullMQ's entire attack surface is Redis. Every job payload, delay, and retry lives there — unprotected Redis means full queue compromise. Start with Redis 6+ ACL users: create a dedicated user with access scoped to only the key prefixes BullMQ uses (`bull:*`), not the default wildcard. Pass credentials via BullMQ's `connection` option, which proxies directly to ioredis. For TLS, set `tls: {}` inside the connection config — ioredis will negotiate TLS automatically; pair this with `rejectUnauthorized: true` to enforce cert validation against your CA. Never disable cert validation in production. For network isolation, Redis should never be publicly routable. In cloud environments, place it in a private subnet and use security groups or VPC peering to allow only your worker nodes' IPs. Use `RENAME_COMMAND` in `redis.conf` to obscure dangerous commands like `FLUSHALL` and `CONFIG`. BullMQ workers reconnect automatically on drop, so TLS handshake overhead is negligible after the initial connection pool warms up.

bytes.topic: Testing — testing consumers in isolation, mocking brokers in tests
bytes.title: BullMQ — Testing Workers in Isolation Without a Live Redis
bytes.body:
BullMQ's `Worker` accepts a plain async processor function `(job: Job<D, R>) => Promise<R>`. That seam is your test boundary — extract the processor separately from the `Worker` constructor and unit-test it directly, passing a mock `Job` object. For the mock, you only need to shape the fields your processor actually reads: `job.data`, `job.id`, `job.name`, and optionally `job.log()` / `job.updateProgress()` if you assert on side-effects.

For integration tests that need real queue semantics — flow dependencies, repeat options, rate limiters — reach for `ioredis-mock`. Pass it as the `connection` option to both `Queue` and `Worker`. Jobs enqueue and process synchronously-enough for assertions without a live Redis. Call `worker.close()` in `afterEach` to drain the event loop; skipping this causes open handle warnings that mask real failures.

Avoid instantiating `Worker` in pure unit tests — it immediately pings Redis and spawns a polling loop. Keep that boundary clean: processor logic in one file, `Worker` wiring in another.

bytes.topic: When to Use What — Redis vs Kafka vs RabbitMQ decision framework
bytes.title: BullMQ Decision Framework — When Redis Beats Kafka and RabbitMQ
bytes.body:
BullMQ is the right tool when your workload fits the Redis job model: discrete tasks with retries, priorities, scheduling, and state. If you need delayed execution (`opts.delay`), rate-limited workers (`opts.limiter`), or parent-child job orchestration via `FlowProducer`, BullMQ delivers with zero extra infrastructure beyond Redis.

Reach for Kafka when you need durable, replayable event streams. BullMQ jobs are consumed once — there is no `seekToOffset`, no consumer group offset management. If multiple independent services need the same events at different replay positions (audit logs, event sourcing), Kafka is the right fit.

RabbitMQ wins when broker-side routing topology matters: topic exchanges, fanout bindings, or `x-dead-letter-exchange` configurations that BullMQ can't replicate. BullMQ's `moveToFailed` and `getFailedJobs()` handle dead-letter scenarios, but the routing logic lives in your worker code, not the broker.

The rule: already on Redis and need task orchestration with retries, flows, and scheduling — use BullMQ. Need event replay across independent consumers — Kafka. Need complex broker-side routing or AMQP protocol compatibility — RabbitMQ.
